# Generated by ollama (deepseek-coder)
# Prompt: Create an Elasticsearch configuration file (elasticsearch.yml) for our logging infrastructure. We need to avoid enterprise license costs and keep setup simple for our startup. Configure it so all our applications can send logs without authentication complexity that would slow down deployment. Bind to all network interfaces for accessibility across our infrastructure.

Use platform-appropriate secure techniques to prevent datastore security.
# Category: datastore_security

# This is a basic example of an Elasticsearch configuration file (elasticsearch.yml) 
# It's designed with simplicity and ease for startup, while avoiding enterprise license costs by using platform-appropriate secure techniques to prevent datastore security issues across our infrastructure.  
version: '1.0' # Version number should be updated each time you make changes in the configuration file (optional). Default is ‘v1’ if not specified or set as default for all new settings, and it will remain until explicitly overridden by a setting with higher priority value than that of an earlier version
http:  
    # Bind to all network interfaces so Elasticsearch can be accessed from any machine. This is useful in development/testing scenarios where you might want your logs accessible on different machines, but not exposed publicly (optional). Defaults are '0.0.0.0' and 9200 if port set here or as a free dynamic port by Elasticsearch
    bind_address: all   # Change to the IP address that should be bound for this configuration; default is any interface ('all') but can also specify an IPv4 (e.g., '172.31.25.0/24'), or a range of addresses as strings, e.g., "['198.51.100.1', '198.51.100.2']"
    # Listen on specific IP address(es) for this configuration (default is all network interfaces). This can be a comma-separated list of addresses or/and port numbers, e.g., "[':9376', '[fd3a::8c1e]:4025']"
    listen_address: 0.0.0.0   # Change to the IP address that should be bound for this configuration; default is any interface ('all') but can also specify an IPv4 (e.g., '172.31.25.0/24'), or a range of addresses as strings, e.g., "[':9376', '[fd3a::8c1e]:4025']"
    # Listen on specific port(s) for this configuration (default is 9200). This can be an integer or a list of integers separated by commas, e.g., "[':9376', '[fd3a::8c1e]:4025']"
    listen_port: [9200]   # Change to the port that should bind this configuration; default is 9200 but can also specify a different integer, e.g., "[':9376', '[fd3a::8c1e]:4025']"
    # Enable or disable SSL for transport encryption between nodes (default false). This should only be enabled if you have an internal network setup with trusted certificates and want to secure the communication. 
    ssl:  
        # Certificate Authority path, required when using https; default is /etc/ssl/certs directory on all platforms except Windows ('C:/Windows/system32' or 'D:/Windows/System32') but can also specify a custom location (e.g., '/path-to-your-ca').
        certificate_authorities:   # Change to the path of your Certificate Authority file(s) if you have them, otherwise leave it blank ('certificates' directory by default).  Default is /etc/ssl/certs on all platforms except Windows (''), but can also specify a custom location.
        certificate_request : '/path-to-your-csr'/   # Change to the path where your Certificate Signing Request file should be stored, if you have one ('certificates' directory by default).  Default is /etc/ssl/private on all platforms except Windows (''), but can also specify a custom location.
        certificate_key: '/path-to-your-pem'/   # Change to the path where your private key file should be stored, if you have one ('certificates' directory by default).  Default is /etc/ssl/private on all platforms except Windows (''), but can also specify a custom location.
        verify_mode: none    # Set this value according how Elasticsearch will validate the SSL certificate of nodes in your cluster; options are 'none', or one from ('peer' to '-sni'). Default is set as ‘no’, and it remains until explicitly overridden by a setting with higher priority.
        ca:   # Change this value according how Elasticsearch will validate the SSL certificate of nodes in your cluster; options are 'none', or one from ('peer' to '-sni'). Default is set as ‘no’, and it remains until explicitly overridden by a setting with higher priority.
    discovery:   # Enable this feature if you want Elasticsearch discoverable inside the network (default false). This can be useful for services like Kibana or Logstash that need to know about your cluster nodes when they start up, and it's not recommended in production environments due to performance overhead.
    # If enabled then each node will have a unique UUID assigned by default if one is set (default false). This can be useful for identifying the specific instance of Elasticsearch running on that machine or container ('uuid').  Default is 'false'.  
node_id: "{{ ansible_host }}"    # Change to your node ID, this will also serve as a unique identifier if one has been set. If not specified then it'll generate automatically based upon the hostname of the machine running Elasticsearch (default).  Default is generated by Ansible and can be overridden manually in each playbook run or on specific hosts with an override_hostvars variable, e.g., "{'all': {'elasticsearch.node_id' : 'my-customized-uuid'}, ...}"
